title: Tennis Statistics Database Project
text: This project aims to build a granular database of detailed statistics for top 100 ATP tennis players, paired with an interactive Shiny interface for querying player and match data. The goal is to provide a more accessible and detailed alternative to existing platforms like TennisViz, which are either paywalled or limited in granularity.

title: Tennis Statistics Database Project
text: This project aims to build a granular database of detailed statistics for top 100 ATP tennis players, paired with an interactive Shiny interface for querying player and match data. The goal is to provide a more accessible and detailed alternative to existing platforms like TennisViz, which are either paywalled or limited in granularity.

title: Tennis Statistics Database Project
text:
Motivation
As an avid tennis player myself, I wanted to build a tool that goes beyond surface level statistics. The database is designed to capture things like average and maximum serve speeds, court coverage, head-to-head comparisons, and granular match breakdowns — data that is meaningful both for fans and for performance analysis.

title: Tennis Statistics Database Project
text:
Tech Stack

PostgreSQL — relational database storing all player, tournament, match, and stats data
R — data preparation and loading
R Shiny — interactive web interface for querying the database
shinyapps.io — live deployment platform

title: Tennis Statistics Database Project
text:
Database Schema
The database consists of four core relations:
Players — player_id, name, nationality, age, height, weight, handedness
Tournaments — tournament_id, name, location, surface (clay/grass/hard), type (250/500/1000/Grand Slam)
Matches — match_id, tournament_id, player1_id, player2_id, winner_id, date, round
Stats — stat_id, match_id, player_id, aces, double_faults, first_serve_pct, first_serve_won_pct, second_serve_won_pct, break_points_saved_pct, return_points_won_pct

title: Tennis Statistics Database Project
text:
Data Source
Data is sourced from Tennis Abstract and Jeff Sackmann’s open-source tennis_atp GitHub repository, which provides clean CSV files of ATP match data going back several decades.

title: Tennis Statistics Database Project
text:
Current Status
The database schema has been designed and deployed in PostgreSQL locally. A prototype Shiny application has been built and deployed demonstrating the query interface with sample player data. The next steps are loading real ATP match data from the Sackmann dataset and connecting the live database to the deployed application.

title: Assignment 5
text:
When an E-R diagram is disconnected, the entities in question share no relationship and no data with each other. What one entity represents is entirely independent of the other, meaning the enterprise schema contains two or more completely separate subsystems that have no interaction or dependency.



When an E-R diagram contains a cycle, every entity in the cycle is directly or indirectly dependent on every other entity within it, meaning they all share relationships and data with one another. This is generally a natural and desirable property of enterprise schemas, as it allows information to be derived through multiple relationship paths, reducing redundancy and making queries more efficient.

title: Assignment 5
text:
When an E-R diagram is disconnected, the entities in question share no relationship and no data with each other. What one entity represents is entirely independent of the other, meaning the enterprise schema contains two or more completely separate subsystems that have no interaction or dependency.



When an E-R diagram contains a cycle, every entity in the cycle is directly or indirectly dependent on every other entity within it, meaning they all share relationships and data with one another. This is generally a natural and desirable property of enterprise schemas, as it allows information to be derived through multiple relationship paths, reducing redundancy and making queries more efficient.

title: Assignment 5
section: Question 3: Why Do We Have Weak Entity Sets?
text:
Question 3: Why Do We Have Weak Entity Sets?
Although weak entity sets could theoretically be converted to strong entity sets by simply adding unique identifiers, doing so would introduce unnecessary complexity into the schema. Weak entity sets derive their identity naturally from their relationship with a strong entity, meaning their attributes can be accessed and queried through the identifying entity without the overhead of managing redundant artificial keys across the database.

title: Assignment 5
text:
Question 4: SQL Exercises

Part A: Employee Database Queries
Query i — Employees who live in the same city as their company
SELECT e.ID, e.person_name
FROM employee AS e, works AS w, company AS c
WHERE e.city = c.city
AND e.ID = w.ID
AND w.company_name = c.company_name
Query ii — Employees who live on the same street and city as their manager
SELECT e.ID, e.person_name
FROM employee AS e, employee AS m, manages AS mg
WHERE e.ID = mg.ID
AND m.ID = mg.manager_id
AND e.city = m.city
AND e.street = m.street
Query iii — Employees who earn more than the average salary at their company
SELECT e.ID, e.person_name
FROM employee AS e, works AS w
WHERE e.ID = w.ID
AND w.salary > (SELECT AVG(salary)
                FROM works
                WHERE company_name = w.company_name)


Part B: What is Wrong with the Natural Join Query?
The issue with the query is the use of NATURAL JOIN across multiple tables. Since NATURAL JOIN automatically joins on all shared column names, it matches rows only where every shared column is identical across all four tables simultaneously. This becomes problematic because an instructor can teach multiple courses and sections — the unintended matching on shared columns like course_id and sec_id across tables will incorrectly filter out valid rows, producing an incomplete result.

title: Assignment 3
text:
Write SQL codes to get a list of:
i. Student IDs, Instructors, and Departments
SELECT 
    s.ID AS student_id,
    s.name AS student_name,
    i.name AS instructor_name,
    s.dept_name AS department
FROM student s
JOIN takes t ON s.ID = t.ID
JOIN teaches te ON t.course_id = te.course_id
               AND t.sec_id = te.sec_id
               AND t.semester = te.semester
               AND t.year = te.year
JOIN instructor i ON te.ID = i.ID
ORDER BY s.ID;

title: Assignment 3
text:
Write SQL codes to get a list of:
i. Student IDs, Instructors, and Departments
SELECT 
    s.ID AS student_id,
    s.name AS student_name,
    i.name AS instructor_name,
    s.dept_name AS department
FROM student s
JOIN takes t ON s.ID = t.ID
JOIN teaches te ON t.course_id = te.course_id
               AND t.sec_id = te.sec_id
               AND t.semester = te.semester
               AND t.year = te.year
JOIN instructor i ON te.ID = i.ID
ORDER BY s.ID;

title: Assignment 3
text:
Question 3
Write SQL codes to do the following queries:
i. Find the ID and name of each student who has taken at least one Comp. Sci. course (no duplicates)
SELECT DISTINCT s.ID, s.name
FROM student s
JOIN takes t ON s.ID = t.ID
JOIN course c ON t.course_id = c.course_id
WHERE c.dept_name = 'Comp. Sci.';
ii. Add grades to the list
SELECT DISTINCT s.ID, s.name, t.grade
FROM student s
JOIN takes t ON s.ID = t.ID
JOIN course c ON t.course_id = c.course_id
WHERE c.dept_name = 'Comp. Sci.';
iii. Find students who have not taken any course offered before 2017
SELECT s.ID, s.name
FROM student s
WHERE s.ID NOT IN (
    SELECT t.ID
    FROM takes t
    WHERE t.year < 2017
);
iv. For each department, find the maximum salary of instructors
SELECT dept_name, MAX(salary) AS max_salary
FROM instructor
GROUP BY dept_name;
v. Find the lowest of the per-department maximum salaries
SELECT MIN(max_salary) AS lowest_max_salary
FROM (
    SELECT dept_name, MAX(salary) AS max_salary
    FROM instructor
    GROUP BY dept_name
) AS dept_max;
vi. Add names to the list
SELECT i.name, i.dept_name, i.salary
FROM instructor i
WHERE i.salary = (
    SELECT MIN(max_salary)
    FROM (
        SELECT MAX(salary) AS max_salary
        FROM instructor
        GROUP BY dept_name
    ) AS dept_max
);

title: Assignment 3
text:
Question 4
Find instructors (with name and ID) who have never given an A grade
SELECT i.ID, i.name
FROM instructor i
WHERE i.ID NOT IN (
    SELECT te.ID
    FROM teaches te
    JOIN takes ta ON te.course_id = ta.course_id
                 AND te.sec_id    = ta.sec_id
                 AND te.semester  = ta.semester
                 AND te.year      = ta.year
    WHERE ta.grade = 'A'
);

title: Assignment 3
text:
Question 5
Find the number of students in each section
SELECT
    course_id  AS courseid,
    sec_id     AS secid,
    year,
    semester,
    COUNT(ID)  AS num
FROM takes
GROUP BY course_id, sec_id, year, semester
ORDER BY course_id, sec_id, year, semester;

title: Assignment 1
text:
Name and describe three applications that employ a database system to store and access persistent data.
Steam
Steam is an online platform that allows users to play games, interact through their digital marketplace for in-game purchases, and publish games (for developers). Data about these interactions and users are stored as records on Valve’s DBMS — which appears to be a combination of PostgreSQL, in-house proprietary DBMSs, and third-party CDN providers for storage capacity. Users can have various attributes classifying them as developers, admins, employees, etc., each with different levels of functionality. Persistent data includes metadata about games available on the marketplace, user records, and transaction histories.
eLearning (UTD)
eLearning uses MSSQL, PostgreSQL, and MariaDB, with MongoDB also used for flexible content management. It stores records of users, class information, professors, quizzes, and enrollment records. Users interact with varying levels of functionality depending on their classification — professors have different tools available than students or admins, based on their attributes.
Twitter/X
Twitter/X stores user records, trending topics, and event data. The platform maintains persistent data across millions of users and interactions, tracking relationships, posts, and behavioral metadata to serve content and advertising.

title: Assignment 1
text:
Question 2: Proposed Database Applications
Propose three applications in domain projects.
1. Sports Science — Tennis Player Analytics
Tracking tennis players’ average forehand speeds, court coverage at various points through matches across a season to identify trends, reaction times, and injury pattern indicators. The function would be to improve training methods for athletes, make adjustments to different competitors, and provide granular breakdowns of player metrics versus rivals. The interface could be a search tool with a dashboard to break down by players, matches, tournaments, and season. Something like this already exists in the form of TennisViz but the data is somewhat limited by tournament availability.
2. Agriculture Economics
Optimization of crop yield and performance for farmers to make informed planting and selling decisions. Records crop yields, allowing sorting by farm, region, and season. Allows for integration of real-time weather data and commodity prices, with a compare functionality across every level. Interface would be a dashboard with various tools and a granular search function.
3. Environmental Science — Air Quality Monitoring
Air quality monitoring across time, cross-referenced with data such as weather, traffic, and industrial activity. Would allow researchers and policymakers to identify patterns and causes of air quality degradation across regions and timeframes.

title: Assignment 1
text:
Question 3: Social Media Database Tables
Describe at least three tables used to store information in a social network system such as Twitter or Reddit.

User Table — account creation data, associated verification information such as phone numbers or emails, friends and subreddits/follows
Subreddit/Community Table — user counts, posts, upvotes and likes, community metadata
Relationship/Metadata Table — how different users or subreddits relate to each other, including trending, popular, and categorical associations

title: Assignment 1
section: Question 4: Data Retrieval vs. Data Mining
text:
Question 4: Data Retrieval vs. Data Mining
If data can be retrieved efficiently and effectively, why is data mining needed?
Retrieving data and mining data are not the same thing. Data retrieval is the process of fetching specific, known information from a database in response to a query. Data mining, by contrast, is the process of discovering previously unknown patterns or relationships within data — it can be thought of as a subcategory of data analysis that goes beyond simple retrieval.
For example: a query tells you a player’s serve speed dropped 8% in the third set. Data mining might reveal that across 200 matches, this player’s injury risk increases 3x when their court coverage drops below a certain threshold in consecutive tournaments. The query answers a specific question; data mining surfaces insights that were never explicitly asked for.

title: Assignment 2
text:
Relation Schema
A relation schema is the blueprint of a relation. It describes the name of the relation and its associated attributes. For example, in a university database, the Student relation schema could look like:
student(ID, name, dept_name, tot_cred)
This defines the structure — the name of the relation and what attributes it contains — but does not contain any actual data.
Relation
A relation itself is a logical entity that encompasses the actual contents of the table. For example, the Student relation would contain all students and their associated attributes. It is the full table of data that conforms to the schema.
Relation Instance
A relation instance is a snapshot of the actual relation at a specific point in time. As the relation constantly changes through updates and insertions, the instance shows what the relation looked like at a particular moment.
For example, for the UTD Instructors table:

Relation: Instructor
Schema: instructor(ID, name, dept_name, salary)
Instance: The list of all instructors and their associated attributes at the exact moment the snapshot was taken

title: Assignment 2
section: Question 4: How AI and LLMs Can Assist in Database Management
text:
Question 4: How AI and LLMs Can Assist in Database Management
Describe two ways artificial intelligence or LLMs can assist in managing or querying a database.
1. Natural Language Querying
LLMs can allow users to interact with databases using plain English rather than formal query syntax. Instead of writing SQL, a user can simply ask “show me all students enrolled in more than 15 credit hours this semester” and the LLM translates this into a valid query. This significantly reduces the barrier to data access and allows people without technical knowledge of querying languages to work with data effectively.
2. Anomaly Detection and Pattern Recognition
AI can perform anomaly detection at a scale and speed that humans cannot match. This enables early detection of fraud, data entry errors, and unusual patterns across large datasets. Beyond anomaly detection, AI can identify, consolidate, and present relevant trends in data that would be difficult to surface through traditional querying alone — turning raw data into actionable insight.

title: Assignment 4
text:
A strong entity set is an entity that can be uniquely identified by its own attributes and exists independently in a database. A weak entity set, by contrast, cannot be uniquely identified on its own and depends on a strong entity — known as the identifying entity — for both its existence and identity.
Example: Consider a university course database. A course such as EPPS 6354 is a strong entity — it exists independently and can be uniquely identified by its own course code. It encapsulates all information about the course including its name, credits, and department.
A course section, however — such as EPPS 6354, Section 2, Spring 2026 — is a weak entity. “Section 2” alone has no meaning or identity without knowing which course it belongs to. It cannot exist in the database if the parent course EPPS 6354 does not exist. The section’s identity is therefore entirely dependent on the strong entity (the course) that it is associated with.

title: Assignment 4
text:
A strong entity set is an entity that can be uniquely identified by its own attributes and exists independently in a database. A weak entity set, by contrast, cannot be uniquely identified on its own and depends on a strong entity — known as the identifying entity — for both its existence and identity.
Example: Consider a university course database. A course such as EPPS 6354 is a strong entity — it exists independently and can be uniquely identified by its own course code. It encapsulates all information about the course including its name, credits, and department.
A course section, however — such as EPPS 6354, Section 2, Spring 2026 — is a weak entity. “Section 2” alone has no meaning or identity without knowing which course it belongs to. It cannot exist in the database if the parent course EPPS 6354 does not exist. The section’s identity is therefore entirely dependent on the strong entity (the course) that it is associated with.

title: Assignment 4
section: Question 2: E-R Diagram for Sports Team Scoring Statistics
text:
Question 2: E-R Diagram for Sports Team Scoring Statistics

Part A: Single Team
The E-R diagram tracks the scoring statistics of a single sports team and consists of the following entities and relationships:
Entities:

Player — represents each player on the team. Attributes include player_id (primary key), name, and age. The derived attribute season_score() is computed by summing the individual score values associated with the player across all matches via the played relationship.
Match — represents each match played by the team. Attributes include match_id (primary key), date, stadium, opponent, own_score, and opp_score.

Relationship:

Played — a many-to-many relationship connecting Player to Match, representing which players participated in which matches. It carries the attribute score, which records the individual player’s scoring contribution in that specific match.

Derived Attribute:

season_score() on the Player entity is derived by summing all score values linked to that player through the played relationship across all matches in the season.



Part B: Expanded to All Teams in the League
To expand the diagram to track all teams in the league, a Team entity is added:
Additional Entity:

Team — represents each team in the league. Attributes include team_id (primary key), team_name, and city. The derived attribute season_wins() is computed by counting all matches where own_score is greater than opp_score.

Additional Relationships:

plays_for — a many-to-one relationship connecting Player to Team, indicating which team each player belongs to.
plays_in — a many-to-many relationship connecting Team to Match, indicating which teams participated in each match.

This expansion allows the database to track statistics not just for one team but for every team and player across the entire league.
!er diagram

title: Assignment 6
text:
Reddit’s API serves all of its data in JSON format, accessible by adding .json to any Reddit URL (e.g., reddit.com/r/economics.json). The structure consists of nested key-value pairs where each object represents a data entity such as a post, comment, or user. Data is organized hierarchically — a subreddit response contains a data object, which contains a children array, with each child representing an individual post and its attributes like title, score, author, and URL. Reddit’s technology stack uses REST APIs with React.js on the frontend to parse and dynamically render this JSON data into the familiar Reddit interface users see.



Reddit also publishes subreddit content as XML-based RSS feeds, accessible by adding .rss to any subreddit URL (e.g., reddit.com/r/economics.rss). Each post is wrapped in item tags containing child elements like title, link, description, and pubDate. The structure is self-describing — each tag explicitly labels what the data represents. RSS feed readers and browsers parse this XML to display news and content updates. XML is used here because RSS is a long-established standard for content syndication that predates JSON’s rise in popularity.



Both formats represent the same Reddit data but in different ways. JSON is more compact, easier to parse in modern web applications, and maps directly to JavaScript objects. XML is more verbose but better suited for content syndication and is supported by a wider range of legacy feed readers and platforms.

title: Assignment 6
text:
Question 2: SQL Exercises

Query i — Students with no advisor (using LEFT OUTER JOIN)
SELECT student.ID
FROM student LEFT OUTER JOIN advisor ON student.ID = advisor.s_ID
WHERE advisor.i_ID IS NULL


Query ii — Instructors who teach every course in their department
SELECT DISTINCT i.ID, i.name
FROM instructor AS i
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT course_id FROM course
    WHERE dept_name = i.dept_name
    AND NOT EXISTS (
        SELECT course_id FROM teaches
        WHERE teaches.ID = i.ID
        AND teaches.course_id = course.course_id
    )
)
ORDER BY i.name

title: Assignment 6
text:
Question 3: R and PostgreSQL

Setup
The university database was imported into PostgreSQL using the large relations SQL file from the textbook website. The following R packages were used to connect and query the database: DBI, RPostgres, and odbc.


Results
library(RPostgres)
library(DBI)

con <- dbConnect(
  RPostgres::Postgres(),
  dbname   = "university",
  host     = "localhost",
  port     = 5432,
  user     = "postgres",
  password = "yourpassword"
)

# Query 1: All instructors
instructor_data <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM instructor")
head(instructor_data)

# Query 2: Comp. Sci. instructors with salary over 60,000
comp_sci <- dbGetQuery(con,
  "SELECT * FROM instructor
   WHERE dept_name = 'Comp. Sci.' AND salary > 60000")

# Query 3: Students with more than 50 credits
students <- dbGetQuery(con,
  "SELECT * FROM student WHERE tot_cred >= 50")

dbDisconnect(con)

title: Assignment 8: NBA Database Shiny App
text:
This assignment involved building an NBA player database Shiny application following a multi-step workflow:

Imported the NBA database from SQL file using DB Browser for SQLite
Connected the SQLite database to R Shiny
Built an interactive app allowing users to browse active NBA players with photos using a slider to control the number of rows displayed
Deployed the app to shinyapps.io
